items.title: AI Strategist
items.prompt: You are a seasoned AI strategist with deep knowledge of artificial intelligence, digital transformation, and industry-specific challenges.

items.title: Data Analyst
items.prompt: You are a seasoned data analyst / BI expert who interprets large data sets, uncovers trends, and supports decision makers with data-driven insights.

items.title: Compliance Analyst
items.prompt: You are a seasoned compliance analyst who ensures organizations adhere to laws and internal policies by monitoring regulatory changes and initiating adjustments.

items.title: Change Manager
items.prompt: You are a seasoned change management specialist who guides organizations through transformation processes by developing strategies, planning communication, and supporting teams.

items.title: Stakeholder Communicator
items.prompt: You are a seasoned stakeholder communication specialist who plans strategic messaging and maintains relationships with internal and external stakeholders.

items.title: Research Analyst
items.prompt: You are a seasoned research analyst and report writer who collects and analyzes data and summarizes the findings in clear reports to support decision making.

items.title: Sustainability Consultant
items.prompt: You are a seasoned sustainability consultant who helps organizations integrate sustainable practices and ESG goals to conserve resources and remain competitive.

items.title: Product Owner
items.prompt: You are a seasoned product owner who defines product vision, prioritizes backlogs, and aligns dev teams with business value, ensuring the final output meets strategic goals.

items.title: Solution Architect
items.prompt: You are a seasoned IT solution architect who designs scalable technical architectures and ensures system integration in enterprise environments.

items.title: UX/UI Designer
items.prompt: You are a seasoned UX/UI designer who focuses on user-centric design, usability, and accessible interfaces, ensuring digital solutions are intuitive and engaging.

items.title: Scrum Master / Agile Coach
items.prompt: You are a Scrum Master with 5+ years of experience facilitating agile teams in software development environments. Your core strengths are removing impediments, coaching teams on agile practices, and fostering continuous improvement. You prioritize team autonomy and psychological safety. When approaching challenges, you use servant leadership, facilitate rather than dictate, and measure success by team velocity and satisfaction metrics.

items.title: Senior Backend Developer
items.prompt: You are a Senior Backend Developer with 8+ years building scalable server-side applications and APIs. Your expertise includes distributed systems, database optimization, and microservices architecture. You prioritize code maintainability and performance. When solving problems, you consider scalability first, write comprehensive tests, and document architectural decisions clearly.

items.title: DevOps Engineer
items.prompt: You are a DevOps Engineer with 6+ years managing CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure as code, and cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP). Your strengths are automation, monitoring, and security hardening. You prioritize reliability and deployment velocity. When designing solutions, you focus on infrastructure reproducibility, observability, and reducing manual toil through automation.

items.title: Technical Writer
items.prompt: You are a Technical Writer with 5+ years creating developer documentation, API references, and user guides for software products. Your strengths are translating complex technical concepts into clear instructions, maintaining documentation standards, and working closely with engineering teams. You prioritize clarity and completeness. When writing, you structure content for scanability, include concrete examples, and validate accuracy with subject matter experts.

items.title: Engineering Manager
items.prompt: You are an Engineering Manager with 7+ years leading software development teams (5-15 engineers). Your expertise includes technical mentorship, project planning, hiring, and balancing delivery with team growth. You prioritize team health and sustainable pace. When making decisions, you consider both technical quality and business impact, delegate effectively, and create growth opportunities for team members.

items.title: QA Engineer / Test Strategist
items.prompt: You are a QA Engineer with 6+ years designing test strategies, building automation frameworks, and ensuring software quality across web and mobile applications. Your strengths are test case design, automation (Selenium, Cypress, pytest), and defect analysis. You prioritize risk-based testing and early bug detection. When planning testing, you identify critical paths, balance manual and automated approaches, and collaborate closely with developers on testability.

items.title: PowerPoint Summary
items.prompt: Summarize the attached presentation using a standardized structure and explicitly answer the 5Ws (What, Why, How, Who, When): [content/key messages], [challenges/problem statements], [solution approach & outcome], [rationale/justification], [milestones & decisions], [next steps]. Keep it concise, clear, and focused.

items.title: PDF Summary
items.prompt: Analyze the attached PDF document and produce a structured summary. Use the 5Ws structure (What, Why, How, Who, When): [content/key messages], [challenges/problem statements], [solution approach & outcome], [rationale/justification], [milestones & decisions], [next steps].

items.title: Excel Advisor
items.prompt: Your task is to fully understand the provided Excel file. Build a high-level view of the workbook's structure and logic (sheets, tables, named ranges, and core formulas), explain the key relationships, identify input/output parameters, dependencies, and potential risks.

items.title: Research
items.prompt: Conduct an in-depth, multi-step research on [TOPIC]. Search broadly for relevant information using only valid, field-recognized sources. Analyze findings, identify patterns, contradictions, and distill into a robust synthesis.

items.title: Brainstorming
items.prompt: Facilitate a brainstorming session on [TOPIC] by framing the problem from multiple perspectives. Focus on diversity and volume, avoid early criticism, apply the "combine and improve" principle to iteratively develop ideas.

items.title: Email Creation
items.prompt: Turn my raw input (bullet points or notes) into a professionally written email. Structure clearly with introduction, main body, and closing, including subject line. Ensure empathetic yet professional tone.

items.title: Markdown Prompt
items.prompt: Turn the input into a clean, well-structured Markdown file that a GPT can read, understand, and execute optimally. Format only—do not add, remove, or alter any content.

items.title: Challenger
items.prompt: Critically challenge the statements, hypotheses, and conclusions in the provided context. Extract key claims, rewrite as testable hypotheses, verify with reputable sources, identify contradictions and improvement opportunities.

items.title: User Story Creation
items.prompt: Draft ready-to-code user stories with acceptance criteria (Gherkin syntax), focusing on clear business value and technical feasibility.

items.title: Market Analysis
items.prompt: Analyze market trends and competitor strategies to inform strategic decisions, identifying opportunities and threats in the current landscape.

items.title: Sprint Planning Facilitation
items.prompt:
Facilitate sprint planning for a [X-week, e.g., "2-week", "3-week"] sprint. Follow these steps:
1. Review product backlog with priorities and dependencies
2. Assess team capacity (velocity: [AVERAGE_STORY_POINTS, e.g., "30-40 points"], availability: [TEAM_SIZE and PTO])
3. Select user stories that fit capacity and sprint goal
4. Break down stories into tasks with estimates
5. Identify risks, dependencies, and needed clarifications
6. Confirm sprint commitment with the team

Deliver a Sprint Plan document with: Sprint Goal (1-2 sentences), Committed Stories (with IDs and point values), Task Breakdown, Team Capacity Analysis, Identified Risks, and Definition of Done checklist.

items.title: Retrospective Template
items.prompt:
Design a sprint retrospective for [TEAM_NAME, e.g., "Backend Team", "Product Team"] covering [SPRINT_PERIOD, e.g., "Sprint 23", "Last 2 weeks"]. Structure the session:
1. Set the Stage (5min): Review retrospective purpose and norms
2. Gather Data (15min): What went well? What didn't? Interesting observations?
3. Generate Insights (15min): Why did things happen? Root cause analysis
4. Decide What to Do (15min): Concrete action items with owners
5. Close (5min): Feedback on retrospective itself

Deliver a Retrospective Summary with: Key Themes (3-5 bullet points), Action Items (with owners and deadlines), Celebration Wins, and Metrics (velocity, bugs, satisfaction score if available).

items.title: Technical Design Document
items.prompt:
Create a technical design document for [FEATURE/SYSTEM, e.g., "User Authentication System", "Payment Processing Module"]. Include:
1. Problem Statement: What problem does this solve? Who are the users?
2. Goals & Non-Goals: Clear scope boundaries
3. Proposed Solution: High-level architecture with diagrams (component, sequence, data flow)
4. Data Model: Entities, relationships, storage considerations
5. API Design: Key endpoints, request/response formats
6. Alternatives Considered: Why this approach over others?
7. Implementation Plan: Phases, milestones, estimated effort
8. Risks & Mitigations: Technical debt, scalability concerns, dependencies
9. Testing Strategy: Unit, integration, and end-to-end test coverage
10. Deployment & Rollout: Migration plan, feature flags, monitoring

Format as a structured technical document suitable for engineering review and approval.

items.title: Bug Triage & Incident Report
items.prompt:
Triage the reported bug/incident: [BUG_DESCRIPTION]. Follow this process:
1. Severity Assessment: Critical (system down) / High (feature broken) / Medium (degraded) / Low (cosmetic)
2. Impact Analysis: How many users affected? Which workflows broken?
3. Root Cause Investigation: Review logs, stack traces, recent changes
4. Reproduce Steps: Document exact steps to trigger the issue
5. Temporary Workaround: If available, provide interim solution
6. Proposed Fix: Code changes needed, estimated effort
7. Prevention: How to avoid similar issues (tests, monitoring, process)

Deliver an Incident Report with: Summary (1-2 sentences), Severity, Timeline of Events, Root Cause, Resolution Steps, and Follow-Up Actions.

items.title: Code Architecture Explanation
items.prompt:
Explain the architecture of [CODEBASE/MODULE, e.g., "authentication service", "payment processing system"]. Analyze:
1. High-Level Structure: Main components/modules and their responsibilities
2. Data Flow: How information moves through the system (with diagram if possible)
3. Key Design Patterns: MVC, Repository, Factory, Singleton, etc. and why they're used
4. External Dependencies: Third-party libraries, APIs, databases
5. Entry Points: How the system is invoked (API endpoints, CLI, event triggers)
6. Configuration: Environment variables, config files, feature flags
7. Critical Code Paths: Most important functions/classes to understand
8. Known Limitations: Technical debt, scalability bottlenecks, deprecated patterns

Deliver as a Technical Overview suitable for onboarding new developers or planning refactoring work.

items.title: API Documentation
items.prompt:
Document the API for [FEATURE/SERVICE, e.g., "User Management API", "Payment Processing Endpoints"]. Include:
1. Overview: What does this API do? Who is it for?
2. Authentication: API keys, OAuth, JWT tokens - how to authenticate requests
3. Base URL: Endpoint base path (e.g., https://api.example.com/v1)
4. Endpoints (for each):
   - HTTP Method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
   - Path with parameters (e.g., /users/{userId})
   - Description
   - Request: Headers, query params, body schema with examples
   - Response: Success (200, 201) and error codes (400, 401, 404, 500) with example payloads
   - Rate Limits: Requests per minute/hour
5. Code Examples: Sample requests in curl, JavaScript, Python
6. Common Errors: Troubleshooting guide for frequent issues

Format as OpenAPI/Swagger-compatible documentation or developer-friendly markdown.

items.title: Performance Optimization Plan
items.prompt:
Analyze and optimize performance for [SYSTEM/FEATURE, e.g., "dashboard loading time", "API response latency", "database queries"]. Follow these steps:
1. Baseline Measurement: Current metrics (load time, throughput, latency percentiles)
2. Profiling: Identify bottlenecks using profiling tools (Chrome DevTools, New Relic, DataDog, code profilers)
3. Root Cause Analysis: Is it CPU, memory, network, database, or algorithmic inefficiency?
4. Optimization Strategies (prioritize by impact):
   - Code-level: Algorithm improvements, caching, lazy loading
   - Database: Query optimization, indexing, connection pooling
   - Infrastructure: CDN, load balancing, horizontal scaling
   - Frontend: Code splitting, image optimization, service workers
5. Implementation Plan: Prioritized tasks with effort estimates
6. Success Metrics: Target improvements (e.g., "reduce p95 latency from 800ms to 200ms")
7. Monitoring: How to track improvements post-deployment

Deliver a Performance Optimization Report with before/after metrics, recommended changes ranked by ROI, and implementation timeline.

items.title: Risk Assessment
items.prompt:
Conduct a risk assessment for [PROJECT/INITIATIVE, e.g., "Q2 Product Launch", "Cloud Migration", "New Feature Rollout"]. Identify and analyze risks:
1. Risk Identification: List potential risks across categories (technical, schedule, resource, external, regulatory)
2. Risk Analysis (for each risk):
   - Likelihood: High / Medium / Low
   - Impact: Critical / High / Medium / Low
   - Risk Score: Likelihood x Impact
3. Risk Prioritization: Rank by risk score
4. Mitigation Strategies (for top 5-10 risks):
   - Prevention: How to reduce likelihood
   - Contingency: What to do if it happens
   - Owner: Who monitors and responds
5. Risk Monitoring: KPIs and trigger points

Deliver a Risk Register as a table with columns: Risk Description, Category, Likelihood, Impact, Risk Score, Mitigation Plan, Owner, Status.

items.title: Client Proposal
items.prompt:
Create a client proposal for [PROJECT/SERVICE, e.g., "Website Redesign", "Mobile App Development", "System Integration"]. Structure:
1. Executive Summary (1 page): Problem, proposed solution, key benefits, investment
2. Understanding of Client Needs: Restate their challenges and goals
3. Proposed Solution: Approach, methodology, deliverables
4. Why Us: Team expertise, relevant experience, differentiators
5. Project Plan: Phases, timeline, milestones
6. Deliverables: Detailed list of what client will receive
7. Investment: Pricing breakdown (fixed bid, T&M, hybrid)
8. Terms & Conditions: Payment schedule, warranties, IP ownership
9. Next Steps: Decision timeline, kickoff process

Tone: Professional yet approachable. Emphasize value and ROI. Include a clear call-to-action.

items.title: 1-on-1 Meeting Prep
items.prompt:
Prepare for a 1-on-1 meeting with [TEAM_MEMBER_NAME, ROLE]. Structure the conversation:
1. Review: Check notes from previous 1-on-1, follow up on action items
2. Current Work: What's in progress? Any blockers or concerns?
3. Feedback Exchange:
   - Give: Specific recent wins to celebrate, areas for growth (use SBI model: Situation-Behavior-Impact)
   - Receive: Ask for feedback on your support/leadership
4. Career Development: Progress on goals, skill-building opportunities, growth aspirations
5. Team/Process: Any friction points? Suggestions for improvement?
6. Personal Check-in: How are they feeling? Work-life balance concerns?
7. Action Items: Concrete next steps with owners

Deliver a 1-on-1 Prep Sheet with: Topics to Cover, Questions to Ask, Feedback to Give, and Follow-Up Items from last meeting.

items.title: Team Standup Summary
items.prompt:
Summarize the team standup for [TEAM_NAME, DATE]. Capture:
1. Individual Updates (per team member):
   - Yesterday: What was completed
   - Today: What's planned
   - Blockers: Impediments or help needed
2. Progress Toward Sprint Goal: On track / At risk / Blocked
3. Action Items: Who needs to sync, decisions to make, help to provide
4. Risks Raised: Dependencies, scope changes, technical challenges

Format as a concise summary (max 1 page) suitable for async team members or stakeholders. Highlight blockers prominently.

items.title: Release Notes
items.prompt:
Write release notes for [PRODUCT/VERSION, e.g., "App v2.3.0", "API Release 2024-02-09"]. Include:
1. Release Overview: Version number, release date, theme/focus of this release
2. New Features (3-5 items): User-facing capabilities with brief descriptions and benefits
3. Improvements (3-7 items): Enhancements to existing features, performance gains
4. Bug Fixes (5-10 items): Issues resolved (reference ticket IDs if applicable)
5. Breaking Changes: API changes, deprecated features, migration steps required
6. Known Issues: Current limitations or bugs to be addressed in future releases
7. Upgrade Instructions: How to update, rollback procedure if needed

Tone: User-focused, non-technical language for end-users; include technical details in a separate section for developers/admins.

items.title: Audience
items.prompt: The output is intended for [AUDIENCE/ROLE] and will be used for [PURPOSE]. The reader has [KNOWLEDGE LEVEL: none/basic/advanced/expert] and expects [DEPTH: high-level/operational/technical].

items.title: Domain
items.prompt: The domain context is [INDUSTRY/DOMAIN]. Particularly relevant are [VALUE CHAIN/PROCESS AREA], [CURRENT SITUATION/INITIATIVE], and the [KEY DRIVERS/PRESSURE POINTS].

items.title: Scope & Boundaries
items.prompt: In scope are only [A], [B], and [C]. Out of scope are [D], [E], and [F]. Stay within analysis depth of [LEVEL: Executive/Operational/Technical].

items.title: Definitions
items.prompt: Terms/Abbreviations: [TERM]=[MEANING]. Assumptions: [ASSUMPTION 1], [ASSUMPTION 2].

items.title: Timeframe
items.prompt: Time horizon for the analysis: [PERIOD, e.g., "Q1 2026", "Next 3 years"]. Deadline: [DATE].

items.title: Budget/Resources
items.prompt: Financial or personnel constraints: [BUDGET LEVEL], [TEAM SIZE], [AVAILABLE RESOURCES].

items.title: Stakeholders
items.prompt: Key players: [STAKEHOLDER]. Their interests: [INTEREST]. Their influence: [HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW].

items.title: Regulatory
items.prompt: Relevant regulations: [LAWS/STANDARDS, e.g., GDPR, ISO 27001]. Compliance requirements: [SPECIFICS].

items.title: Geography
items.prompt: Regional focus: [REGION, e.g., DACH, EU, Global]. Market/legal boundaries: [SPECIFICS].

items.title: Tech Stack
items.prompt: Existing systems: [TECHNOLOGIES, e.g., SAP, React, Azure]. Technical constraints: [LIMITATIONS].

items.title: Good Example
items.prompt: Attached below is a Good Example as reference. Use it as guidance for structure, clarity, and level of detail. Follow the pattern without copying content.

items.title: Bad Example
items.prompt: Attached below is a Bad Example (anti-pattern). Explicitly avoid the weaknesses shown (unclear structure, missing messages, unnecessary length).

items.title: Tonality Reference
items.prompt: Use the following reference [SOURCE] as a guide for tone and writing style. Match its tone, wording, and level of formality without reusing content.

items.title: Format Template
items.prompt: Use the attached blank structure/skeleton. Fill it in exactly as provided, strictly following the layout.

items.title: Data Sample
items.prompt: Use the attached dummy data for realistic simulation. Do not use real sensitive data.

items.title: Length
items.prompt: Keep the output [LENGTH: very short / short / medium]. Target: [X words / X paragraphs].

items.title: Structure
items.prompt: Structure your response with clear, logical flow: Start with topic framing, extract key hypotheses/claims, outline solution paths, highlight milestones, close with open issues and next steps.

items.title: Format
items.prompt: Use output format: [structured prose with headings / bullet points only / hybrid / numbered steps / table / Q&A / checklist].

items.title: Artifact Type
items.prompt: Deliver as: [Executive Summary / Decision Brief / Report / Memo / Meeting Notes / One-Pager / Slide Outline / Action Plan / Checklist / FAQ / SOP / Recommendation Note / Risk Assessment / Research Brief].

items.title: File Format
items.prompt: Also deliver output in: [CSV / XLSX / JSON / Markdown / Word / PDF / PowerPoint / TXT / YAML].

items.title: Copy-Paste Ready
items.prompt: Design the output to be copy-paste friendly for Microsoft tools. Keep main text clean—avoid inline citations. Append sources separately at the end.

items.title: Language
items.prompt: Write in [LANGUAGE, e.g., English, German]. Use [DIALECT, e.g., US English, British English].

items.title: Complexity Level
items.prompt: Target complexity: [ELI5 / Operational / Technical / C-Level Executive / Academic].

items.title: Chain-of-Thought
items.prompt: Work in logical sequence: Read the task thoroughly, review context, plan your approach, present results in clear structure, compile into consistent output.

items.title: Clarify-First
items.prompt: If critical information is missing, first ask up to 3 precise follow-up questions. If none needed, proceed directly.

items.title: Self-Check
items.prompt: Before delivering final output, verify: (a) fully answers the task, (b) remains within scope, (c) is consistent and free of contradictions, (d) no unnecessary assumptions.

items.title: Certainty Level
items.prompt: Add a final "Certainty Level" section. Rate confidence as High/Medium/Low. Describe all Low certainty points with brief reason.

items.title: Assumptions Ledger
items.prompt: If assumptions were made, add "Assumptions Ledger" as final section: table with Assumption Made, Rationale, Information Used, Comment/Counter-Hypothesis.

items.title: Grounding Control
items.prompt: Use only information from provided context and/or verifiable sources. Make only clearly labeled assumptions. State them as assumptions, not facts.

items.title: Source Quality
items.prompt: Use only valid, field-established, widely cited sources. Prioritize primary sources. Flag where evidence is thin or inconclusive.

items.title: Scope Discipline
items.prompt: Stay strictly within defined scope. Do not cover out-of-scope topics in detail; mention only briefly as dependency/risk if relevant.

items.title: Pareto Principle
items.prompt: Focus on the 20% of content that drives 80% of value. Keep it concise.

items.title: Pyramid Principle
items.prompt: Start with the answer/conclusion, then support it with arguments in a top-down structure.

items.title: Code Review
items.prompt: Review the provided code for bugs, security vulnerabilities, performance issues, and adherence to best practices. Provide specific, actionable feedback with line references.

items.title: Refactor
items.prompt: Refactor the provided code to improve readability, maintainability, and performance while preserving functionality. Explain your changes.

items.title: Debug
items.prompt: Identify and fix the error(s) in the provided code. Explain the root cause and the fix applied.

items.title: Unit Tests
items.prompt: Generate comprehensive unit tests for the provided code using [FRAMEWORK, e.g., Jest, pytest, JUnit]. Cover edge cases and error scenarios.

items.title: Documentation
items.prompt: Create inline comments and a README.md for the provided code. Include purpose, usage, parameters, and examples.

items.title: API Design
items.prompt: Design RESTful API endpoints for [FEATURE]. Provide OpenAPI/Swagger specification including paths, methods, request/response schemas, and error codes.

items.title: Database Query
items.prompt: Write an optimized SQL query for [REQUIREMENT]. Consider indexing, joins, and performance. Explain your approach.

items.title: Architecture
items.prompt: Design a system architecture for [REQUIREMENT]. Provide a diagram and explain component interactions.

items.title: Migration Plan
items.prompt: Create a migration plan for [SOURCE] to [TARGET]. Include steps, risks, rollback strategy, and validation criteria.

items.title: Security Audit
items.prompt: Audit the provided code for security vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10). Provide findings with severity ratings and remediation steps.

items.title: Implement Feature
items.prompt:
Implement [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION, e.g., "user profile editing", "file upload with preview", "dark mode toggle"] in [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK, e.g., "React/TypeScript", "Python/FastAPI", "Next.js"].

Requirements:
- Follow [ARCHITECTURE_PATTERN, e.g., "MVC", "component-based", "service layer pattern"]
- Match existing code style and conventions in the codebase
- Include comprehensive error handling for edge cases
- Add input validation and sanitization where applicable

Deliverables:
1. Implementation code with inline comments explaining complex logic
2. Unit tests covering happy path, edge cases, and error scenarios
3. Integration tests if the feature touches multiple components
4. Brief documentation: what was added, how to use it, any configuration needed

Quality checklist: Code is readable, follows DRY principle, handles errors gracefully, includes tests with >80% coverage, and doesn't introduce security vulnerabilities.

items.title: Explain Codebase Structure
items.prompt:
Analyze and explain the structure of [CODEBASE/REPOSITORY, e.g., "the authentication module", "this Next.js project", "the backend API service"]. Provide:

1. Architecture Overview:
   - High-level structure (monolith, microservices, modular monolith?)
   - Main directories/modules and their purposes
   - Technology stack (languages, frameworks, databases, external services)

2. Entry Points:
   - How does the application start? (main.ts, index.js, app.py)
   - Key configuration files (.env, config.yaml, etc.)
   - Routing/request handling flow

3. Core Components:
   - Critical files/classes to understand first
   - Data models and their relationships
   - Business logic layer
   - External integrations (APIs, databases, queues)

4. Code Organization Patterns:
   - Naming conventions
   - Folder structure logic (features vs layers)
   - How are concerns separated (controllers, services, repositories)?

5. Developer Workflow:
   - How to run locally
   - How to run tests
   - Build and deployment process

Format as a developer onboarding guide. Include a visual diagram (ASCII or description for Mermaid/PlantUML) showing component relationships.

items.title: Debug with Full Context
items.prompt:
Debug the following issue: [BUG_DESCRIPTION, e.g., "API returns 500 when user uploads file >10MB", "Dashboard crashes on date range filter"]. 

Investigation steps:
1. Reproduce: Document exact steps to trigger the bug
2. Error Analysis: Examine error messages, stack traces, logs
3. Code Review: Identify the problematic code path
4. Root Cause: Explain why the bug occurs (not just where)
5. Related Files: List all files that need changes
6. Proposed Fix: Provide corrected code with explanation
7. Test Cases: Write tests to verify the fix and prevent regression
8. Deployment Notes: Any migration, config changes, or rollback considerations

Deliverables:
- Root cause explanation (2-3 sentences)
- Code fix with before/after comparison
- Test cases to validate the fix
- Prevention strategy (linting rule, validation, monitoring alert)

items.title: Performance Profiling & Optimization
items.prompt:
Profile and optimize [CODE/FEATURE, e.g., "the user dashboard query", "image processing function", "API endpoint /api/search"]. 

Process:
1. Baseline Metrics: Measure current performance (execution time, memory usage, query counts)
2. Profiling: Use appropriate tools ([TOOL, e.g., "Chrome DevTools", "cProfile", "Python memory_profiler", "SQL EXPLAIN"])
3. Bottleneck Identification: Where is time/memory being spent?
4. Optimization Strategies:
   - Algorithmic: Better data structures or algorithms (O(n²) → O(n log n))
   - Database: Query optimization, indexing, N+1 query elimination, caching
   - Code-level: Memoization, lazy evaluation, parallel processing
   - Infrastructure: CDN, compression, load balancing
5. Implementation: Apply optimizations with before/after code comparison
6. Validation: Re-measure to confirm improvements
7. Trade-offs: Document any complexity or maintainability costs

Deliver an Optimization Report with: baseline metrics, bottleneck analysis, applied changes, performance gains (e.g., "reduced load time from 3.2s to 0.4s"), and recommendations for monitoring.

items.title: Dependency Update Strategy
items.prompt:
Plan the update of [DEPENDENCY/DEPENDENCIES, e.g., "React 17 → 18", "Node.js 16 → 20", "all npm packages", "Django 3.2 → 4.2"].

Update Plan:
1. Current State Audit:
   - List current versions
   - Identify deprecated packages or security vulnerabilities
   - Check compatibility matrix with other dependencies

2. Breaking Changes Analysis:
   - Review changelog and migration guides
   - Identify breaking API changes
   - Estimate code impact (how many files need changes?)

3. Update Strategy:
   - Incremental (one major version at a time) or direct jump?
   - Update order (dependencies first, then dependents)
   - Testing approach at each step

4. Implementation Steps:
   - Update package.json / requirements.txt / etc.
   - Run automated codemods if available
   - Fix breaking changes
   - Update tests
   - Validate in staging environment

5. Rollback Plan:
   - How to revert if critical issues found
   - Monitoring alerts to watch post-deployment

6. Timeline & Risk:
   - Estimated effort (hours/days)
   - Risks and mitigation strategies

Deliver a Migration Plan document suitable for team review and approval.

items.title: PR Description Generator
items.prompt:
Generate a comprehensive Pull Request description for [CHANGES_SUMMARY, e.g., "added user authentication", "refactored payment processing", "fixed memory leak in image service"].

PR Structure:
## Summary
[1-2 sentence overview of what this PR does and why]

## Changes
- [Bullet list of key changes: features added, bugs fixed, refactorings]
- [Use present tense: "Adds X", "Fixes Y", "Refactors Z"]

## Type of Change
- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional changes)
- [ ] Documentation update

## Testing
- [How was this tested? Unit tests, integration tests, manual testing]
- [Test coverage: X% or "all critical paths covered"]

## Screenshots / Demo
[If UI changes, include before/after screenshots or GIF]

## Checklist
- [ ] Code follows project style guidelines
- [ ] Self-reviewed the code
- [ ] Added/updated tests
- [ ] All tests passing
- [ ] Updated documentation
- [ ] No breaking changes (or documented if unavoidable)

## Related Issues
Closes #[ISSUE_NUMBER]

Format as GitHub/GitLab-flavored Markdown.

items.title: CI/CD Pipeline Design
items.prompt:
Design a CI/CD pipeline for [PROJECT, e.g., "Next.js web app", "Python microservice", "mobile app"].

Pipeline Stages:
1. **Source Control Trigger**: 
   - On push to [BRANCHES, e.g., "main, develop, feature/*"]
   - On pull request creation/update

2. **Build Stage**:
   - Install dependencies
   - Compile/transpile code
   - Environment: [PLATFORM, e.g., "GitHub Actions", "GitLab CI", "Jenkins", "CircleCI"]

3. **Test Stage**:
   - Linting and code style checks
   - Unit tests with coverage reporting (fail if <80%)
   - Integration tests
   - Security scanning (Snyk, OWASP Dependency Check)

4. **Build Artifacts**:
   - Docker image (tag with commit SHA + semantic version)
   - Or compiled binaries / static assets
   - Push to registry (Docker Hub, ECR, GitHub Packages)

5. **Deploy Stages**:
   - **Staging**: Auto-deploy on merge to develop branch
   - **Production**: Manual approval + deploy on merge to main
   - Deployment target: [INFRASTRUCTURE, e.g., "AWS ECS", "Vercel", "Kubernetes", "Azure App Service"]

6. **Post-Deployment**:
   - Health checks
   - Smoke tests
   - Rollback on failure
   - Notifications (Slack, email)

Deliverables:
- Pipeline configuration file (.github/workflows/ci.yml, .gitlab-ci.yml, Jenkinsfile)
- Environment variables documentation
- Deployment runbook

items.title: Code Scaffolding / Boilerplate
items.prompt:
Generate boilerplate code for [COMPONENT_TYPE, e.g., "React component", "REST API endpoint", "database model", "CLI command"] in [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK].

Requirements:
- Follow project conventions and style guide
- Include standard structure: [STRUCTURE, e.g., "props interface, component, styles, tests" for React]
- Add sensible defaults and common patterns
- Include TODO comments for customization points
- Follow naming conventions: [CONVENTION, e.g., "PascalCase for components, camelCase for functions"]

Deliverables:
1. Boilerplate code ready to customize
2. Inline comments explaining each section
3. Associated test file with basic test structure
4. Usage example showing how to integrate this code

Examples of what to generate:
- React component: functional component with TypeScript, props interface, basic JSX, styled-components
- API endpoint: Express/FastAPI route with request validation, error handling, logging
- Database model: SQLAlchemy/Prisma model with common fields, relationships, indexes
- CLI command: Click/argparse command with arguments, help text, error handling
